verses.explainer: She says 'The glory has departed from Israel, for the ark of God has been taken.' These are her dying words, and they serve as the epitaph for an entire era.

explainer.summary: The captured ark proves to be anything but a passive trophy. Placed in the temple of Dagon, it causes the Philistine idol to fall prostrate before it -- twice. God then strikes the Philistines with tumors and devastation in every city where the ark is moved. The Philistines discover that capturing God's ark has not given them power over Israel's God; instead, they have brought judgment on themselves.
themes: The ark as God's active presence
The foolishness of trying to control God
explainer.contextInBook: After the devastating events of chapter 4, chapter 5 offers comic relief and theological reassurance. God has not been defeated; he is fighting his own battle in enemy territory. The Philistines captured the ark but not the God of the ark.

wordStudies.translatedAs: A major Philistine deity, likely a grain or fertil
wordStudies.meaning: A major Philistine deity, likely a grain or fertility god (from dagan, 'grain'). Some traditions associate him with a fish deity. His temple at Ashdod was one of the most important Philistine religious sites.

wordStudies.translatedAs: Swellings or tumors, possibly associated with bubo
wordStudies.meaning: Swellings or tumors, possibly associated with bubonic plague (the connection to mice in 6:4-5 suggests a rodent-borne disease). The affliction was both physically painful and ritually defiling.

verses.text: Now the Philistines had taken God’s ark, and they brought it from Ebenezer to Ashdod.
verses.explainer: The Philistines take the captured ark from Ebenezer to Ashdod, one of their five major cities. They treat it as a war trophy, proof of their god's superiority.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: In ancient warfare, capturing an enemy's religious objects was the ultimate victory -- it meant your god had defeated theirs. The Philistines placed the ark in Dagon's temple to demonstrate Dagon's supremacy over Israel's God.

verses.text: The Philistines took God’s ark, and brought it into the house of Dagon, and set it by Dagon.
verses.explainer: They place the ark in the temple of Dagon, setting it beside their idol. In their understanding, Israel's God is now Dagon's prisoner and servant.

crossReferences.connection: Isaiah mocks Babylonian gods who must be carried by their worshipers, while the God of Israel carries his people. The Dagon episode illustrates this same contrast.

verses.text: When the people of Ashdod arose early on the next day, behold, Dagon had fallen on his face to the ground before Yahweh’s ark. They took Dagon, and set him in his place again.
verses.explainer: The next morning, the people of Ashdod find Dagon fallen face-down before the ark. They set him back in his place, interpreting it as an accident.

thenVsNow.then: The image of a supposedly powerful idol toppling before the ark of the true God is both humorous and profound. Every power that sets itself up against God eventually falls on its face.
thenVsNow.now: The image of a supposedly powerful idol toppling before the ark of the true God is both humorous and profound. Every power that sets itself up against God eventually falls on its face.

verses.text: When they arose early on the following morning, behold, Dagon had fallen on his face to the ground before Yahweh’s ark; and the head of Dagon and both the palms of his hands were cut off on the threshold. Only Dagon’s torso was intact.
verses.explainer: The following morning, Dagon has fallen again, but this time his head and hands are broken off on the threshold. Only the trunk of Dagon remains intact.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: The dismemberment of Dagon mirrors ancient victory rituals where the head and hands of defeated enemies were displayed. God is acting as a warrior who has defeated the Philistine god in his own temple.

verses.text: Therefore neither the priests of Dagon, nor any who come into Dagon’s house, step on the threshold of Dagon in Ashdod, to this day.
verses.explainer: This is why the priests of Dagon and visitors do not step on the threshold of Dagon's temple to this day. The event creates a lasting religious practice born from the humiliation of their god.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: Threshold customs were common in ancient Near Eastern religion. This etiological note explains an existing Philistine practice by connecting it to the ark's power. It shows the lasting impact of this event on Philistine religion.

verses.text: But Yahweh’s hand was heavy on the people of Ashdod, and he destroyed them, and struck them with tumors, even Ashdod and its borders.
verses.explainer: The hand of the Lord is heavy on the people of Ashdod. He devastates them and strikes them with tumors. The judgment expands from the temple to the city at large.

crossReferences.connection: The hand of the Lord striking with plagues echoes the exodus. The Philistines are experiencing their own mini-exodus plagues for holding God's ark captive.

verses.text: When the men of Ashdod saw that it was so, they said, “The ark of the God of Israel shall not stay with us; for his hand is severe on us, and on Dagon our god.”
verses.explainer: When the men of Ashdod see what is happening, they declare that the ark of Israel's God must not stay with them because his hand is severe against them and Dagon.

thenVsNow.then: The Philistines reach a conclusion that Israel failed to grasp: God's presence is not something to be manipulated for advantage. It brings judgment on those who are not in right relationship with him.
thenVsNow.now: The Philistines reach a conclusion that Israel failed to grasp: God's presence is not something to be manipulated for advantage. It brings judgment on those who are not in right relationship with him.

verses.text:
They sent therefore and gathered together all the lords of the Philistines, and said, “What shall we do with the ark of the God of Israel?”
They answered, “Let the ark of the God of Israel be carried over to Gath.” They carried the ark of the God of Israel there.
verses.explainer: The lords of the Philistines gather and decide to move the ark to Gath. They pass the problem to another city rather than returning what they took.

verses.text: It was so, that after they had carried it there, Yahweh’s hand was against the city with a very great confusion; and he struck the men of the city, both small and great so that tumors broke out on them.
verses.explainer: After the ark arrives in Gath, the Lord strikes that city too with a great panic and tumors affecting both young and old. The judgment follows the ark wherever it goes.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: The pattern of plague following the ark from city to city mirrors the escalating plagues on Egypt. God is demonstrating to the Philistines that their military victory did not equal spiritual superiority.

verses.text:
So they sent God’s ark to Ekron.
As God’s ark came to Ekron, the Ekronites cried out, saying, “They have brought the ark of the God of Israel here to us, to kill us and our people.”
verses.explainer: They send the ark to Ekron, but the people of Ekron cry out in protest when it arrives, saying the Philistines are trying to kill them with Israel's ark.

verses.text: They sent therefore and gathered together all the lords of the Philistines, and they said, “Send the ark of the God of Israel away, and let it go again to its own place, that it not kill us and our people.” For there was a deadly confusion throughout all the city. The hand of God was very heavy there.
verses.explainer: The Ekronites urgently convene the Philistine lords, demanding the ark be sent back to its own place before it kills them all. A deadly panic has seized the city.

thenVsNow.then: The Philistines learned what many have learned since: God cannot be domesticated, captured, or controlled. His presence is a blessing to those who approach him rightly and a danger to those who treat him as an object to be possessed.
thenVsNow.now: The Philistines learned what many have learned since: God cannot be domesticated, captured, or controlled. His presence is a blessing to those who approach him rightly and a danger to those who treat him as an object to be possessed.

verses.text: The men who didn’t die were struck with the tumors; and the cry of the city went up to heaven.
verses.explainer: Those who do not die are struck with tumors, and the city's cry goes up to heaven. The chapter ends with Philistine cities in chaos, begging to be rid of the ark they once triumphantly captured.

crossReferences.connection: The cry going up to heaven echoes the great cry in Egypt on the night of the Passover. The ark narratives repeatedly parallel the exodus, showing God fighting the same battles in a new generation.

explainer.summary: After seven months, the desperate Philistines consult their priests on how to return the ark. They send it back on a new cart with guilt offerings of golden tumors and golden mice, devising a test to confirm whether Israel's God caused the plagues. The cows pulling the cart go straight to Beth-shemesh against their natural instinct, confirming divine direction. But even the Israelites at Beth-shemesh are struck down for looking into the ark, showing that God's holiness demands reverence from everyone.
themes: Pagan recognition of the true God
The proper way to approach the sacred
explainer.contextInBook: The ark's return journey begins God's reassertion of his presence in Israel. But the Beth-shemesh incident shows that Israel, like the Philistines, must learn to approach God with proper reverence. The ark eventually rests at Kiriath-jearim until David brings it to Jerusalem.

wordStudies.translatedAs: A reparation or compensation offering for sacrileg
wordStudies.meaning: A reparation or compensation offering for sacrilege or trespass against holy things. The Philistine priests instinctively understand that they have committed sacrilege against Israel's God and must make restitution.

wordStudies.translatedAs: Weight, honor, glory
wordStudies.meaning: Weight, honor, glory. When the Philistine priests say 'give glory to the God of Israel,' they mean to acknowledge his power and superiority -- the opposite of the humiliation they intended when capturing the ark.

verses.text: Yahweh’s ark was in the country of the Philistines seven months.
verses.explainer: The ark remains in Philistine territory for seven months, during which the plagues continue. The extended timeframe shows the Philistines' reluctance to return their war trophy.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: Seven months was a significant period -- long enough for the Philistines to exhaust other options before consulting their priests. The number seven also carries symbolic completeness in Semitic cultures.

verses.text: The Philistines called for the priests and the diviners, saying, “What shall we do with Yahweh’s ark? Show us how we should send it to its place.”
verses.explainer: The Philistines call their priests and diviners to ask how to send the ark back. Even pagans recognize the need for expert religious guidance when dealing with sacred objects.

verses.text: They said, “If you send away the ark of the God of Israel, don’t send it empty; but by all means return a trespass offering to him. Then you will be healed, and it will be known to you why his hand is not removed from you.”
verses.explainer: The priests advise not sending the ark back empty but including a guilt offering. They understand the principle of atonement -- you cannot trespass against a deity without making restitution.

crossReferences.connection: The Hebrew guilt offering (asham) was specifically for trespass against holy things. The Philistine priests intuitively grasp a principle formalized in Israelite law.

verses.text:
Then they said, “What should the trespass offering be which we shall return to him?”
They said, “Five golden tumors, and five golden mice, for the number of the lords of the Philistines; for one plague was on you all, and on your lords.
verses.explainer: They prescribe five golden tumors and five golden mice, one for each Philistine lord and city. The gold replicas represent the afflictions being symbolically returned to God.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: Offering golden replicas of afflictions was a common ancient Near Eastern practice. Votive offerings shaped like diseased body parts have been found at temples throughout the Mediterranean world. The Philistines were following their own religious conventions.

verses.text: Therefore you shall make images of your tumors, and images of your mice that mar the land; and you shall give glory to the God of Israel. Perhaps he will release his hand from you, from your gods, and from your land.
verses.explainer: The priests instruct the Philistines to give glory to the God of Israel so that perhaps he will lighten his hand on them. They acknowledge Israel's God as the source of the plague.

annotations.word: give glory to the God of Israel

verses.text: Why then do you harden your hearts, as the Egyptians and Pharaoh hardened their hearts? When he had worked wonderfully among them, didn’t they let the people go, and they departed?
verses.explainer: The priests reference Pharaoh and Egypt, warning against hardening hearts as they did. The exodus story is so well-known that Philistine priests use it as a cautionary tale.

crossReferences.connection: The Philistine priests explicitly compare their situation to Egypt's. They have learned from history what Pharaoh learned too late: resisting Israel's God brings escalating judgment.

thenVsNow.then: Learning from others' mistakes is wisdom. The Philistine priests demonstrate that even outsiders can draw right conclusions from sacred history when they are willing to see the truth.
thenVsNow.now: Learning from others' mistakes is wisdom. The Philistine priests demonstrate that even outsiders can draw right conclusions from sacred history when they are willing to see the truth.

verses.text: “Now therefore take and prepare yourselves a new cart, and two milk cows, on which there has come no yoke; and tie the cows to the cart, and bring their calves home from them;
verses.explainer: They devise a test: put the ark on a new cart pulled by two nursing cows that have never been yoked. If the cows go toward Beth-shemesh, God caused the plague; if not, it was coincidence.

verses.text: and take Yahweh’s ark, and lay it on the cart. Put the jewels of gold, which you return him for a trespass offering, in a coffer by its side; and send it away, that it may go.
verses.explainer: They place the ark on the cart along with a box containing the golden guilt offerings. Every detail is carefully arranged according to the priests' instructions.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: Using a new cart and unyoked cows reflected the ancient principle that sacred objects required vessels that had never been used for common purposes. Even the Philistines understood that holy things demanded fresh, uncontaminated handling.

verses.text: Behold; if it goes up by the way of its own border to Beth Shemesh, then he has done us this great evil; but if not, then we shall know that it is not his hand that struck us. It was a chance that happened to us.”
verses.explainer: The test will work by observation: if the cows go to Beth-shemesh, God did this; if they go elsewhere, it was random chance. The Philistines are honest enough to let the evidence decide.

thenVsNow.then: The Philistines' willingness to design a fair test and accept the outcome shows intellectual honesty. They did not rig the test to get the answer they wanted -- a quality that transcends culture and era.
thenVsNow.now: The Philistines' willingness to design a fair test and accept the outcome shows intellectual honesty. They did not rig the test to get the answer they wanted -- a quality that transcends culture and era.

verses.text: The men did so, and took two milk cows, and tied them to the cart, and shut up their calves at home.
verses.explainer: The Philistines prepare everything as instructed, yoking the nursing cows to the cart and shutting their calves away. The scene is set for the divine test.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: Separating nursing cows from their calves ensured the test was rigorous. Every natural instinct would drive the cows back to their calves, making any movement toward Israel unmistakably supernatural.

verses.text: They put Yahweh’s ark on the cart, and the coffer with the golden mice and the images of their tumors.
verses.explainer: They place the ark and the box of golden offerings on the cart. The Philistines have done everything their priests advised.

verses.text: The cows took the straight way by the way to Beth Shemesh. They went along the highway, lowing as they went, and didn’t turn away to the right hand or to the left; and the lords of the Philistines went after them to the border of Beth Shemesh.
verses.explainer: The cows go straight toward Beth-shemesh, lowing as they go but not turning aside. The Philistine lords follow them to the border. The test is conclusive -- God is directing the cows.

verses.text: The people of Beth Shemesh were reaping their wheat harvest in the valley; and they lifted up their eyes, and saw the ark, and rejoiced to see it.
verses.explainer: The people of Beth-shemesh are harvesting wheat in the valley. They look up, see the ark, and rejoice. The ark has returned to Israelite territory.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: Wheat harvest placed this event in late spring or early summer (May-June). Beth-shemesh was a Levitical city in the Shephelah (foothills), making it a fitting first stop for the returning ark.

verses.text: The cart came into the field of Joshua of Beth Shemesh, and stood there, where there was a great stone. Then they split the wood of the cart, and offered up the cows for a burnt offering to Yahweh.
verses.explainer: The cart comes to the field of Joshua of Beth-shemesh and stops by a large stone. They chop up the cart and offer the cows as a burnt offering to the Lord.

verses.text: The Levites took down Yahweh’s ark, and the coffer that was with it, in which the jewels of gold were, and put them on the great stone; and the men of Beth Shemesh offered burnt offerings and sacrificed sacrifices the same day to Yahweh.
verses.explainer: The Levites take down the ark and the box of gold offerings and set them on the large stone. The people offer burnt offerings and sacrifices to the Lord that day.

crossReferences.connection: The law specified that only Levites could handle the sacred objects of the tabernacle. That Levites are present at Beth-shemesh (a Levitical city) provides proper handling for the ark.

verses.text: When the five lords of the Philistines had seen it, they returned to Ekron the same day.
verses.explainer: The five Philistine lords observe the whole scene and then return to Ekron. They have witnessed the conclusive proof that Israel's God caused the plague.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: The Philistine lords witnessing the ark's return served as official confirmation that the experiment worked. Their return to Ekron without the ark marks the end of the Philistine ark narrative.

verses.text: These are the golden tumors which the Philistines returned for a trespass offering to Yahweh: for Ashdod one, for Gaza one, for Ashkelon one, for Gath one, for Ekron one;
verses.explainer: The narrator lists the five golden tumors corresponding to the five Philistine cities: Ashdod, Gaza, Ashkelon, Gath, and Ekron. Each major city sent its own offering.

verses.text: and the golden mice, according to the number of all the cities of the Philistines belonging to the five lords, both of fortified cities and of country villages, even to the great stone, on which they set down Yahweh’s ark. That stone remains to this day in the field of Joshua of Beth Shemesh.
verses.explainer: The golden mice correspond to all the Philistine cities, both fortified cities and country villages. The plague affected the entire territory, not just the five capitals.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: The distinction between 'fortified cities' and 'country villages' (perazi) shows the plague's reach. Even rural areas were affected, suggesting a widespread epidemic rather than localized incidents.

verses.text: He struck of the men of Beth Shemesh, because they had looked into Yahweh’s ark, he struck fifty thousand seventy of the men. Then the people mourned, because Yahweh had struck the people with a great slaughter.
verses.explainer: God strikes down some men of Beth-shemesh because they looked into the ark. Seventy men die, and the people mourn because the Lord struck them with a great blow.

crossReferences.connection: The law explicitly warned that even the Kohathite Levites must not look at the holy objects or they would die. The Beth-shemesh tragedy was the consequence of violating this command.

thenVsNow.then: Modern readers may struggle with this severity, but the passage makes a vital point: God's holiness is not less dangerous for his own people. Familiarity with sacred things can breed a casualness that is itself a form of disrespect.
thenVsNow.now: Modern readers may struggle with this severity, but the passage makes a vital point: God's holiness is not less dangerous for his own people. Familiarity with sacred things can breed a casualness that is itself a form of disrespect.

verses.text: The men of Beth Shemesh said, “Who is able to stand before Yahweh, this holy God? To whom shall he go up from us?”
verses.explainer: The men of Beth-shemesh ask, 'Who is able to stand before the Lord, this holy God? And to whom shall he go up from us?' They want to send the ark away.

verses.text: They sent messengers to the inhabitants of Kiriath Jearim, saying, “The Philistines have brought back Yahweh’s ark. Come down, and bring it up to yourselves.”
verses.explainer: They send messengers to the people of Kiriath-jearim, asking them to come and take the ark. The ark will rest there for twenty years until David retrieves it.

crossReferences.connection: The ark remains at Kiriath-jearim until David brings it to Jerusalem with great celebration -- and another incident of divine judgment when Uzzah touches it (2 Samuel 6:6-7).

explainer.summary: After twenty years with the ark at Kiriath-jearim, Samuel calls Israel to repentance. The people put away their foreign gods and gather at Mizpah for prayer and fasting. When the Philistines attack during this assembly, God thunders from heaven and routes them. Samuel sets up a memorial stone called Ebenezer -- 'Thus far the Lord has helped us.' Samuel serves as judge over Israel for the rest of his life.
explainer.contextInBook: Chapter 7 is the climax of Samuel's career as judge and the high point of his leadership. Israel's repentance and God's victory at Mizpah demonstrate what happens when the people genuinely turn back to God. But this ideal will soon be challenged by the people's demand for a king in chapter 8.

wordStudies.translatedAs: Stone of help
wordStudies.meaning: Stone of help. Samuel's memorial stone commemorates God's assistance against the Philistines. The name transforms a site of previous defeat (4:1) into a site of victory -- the same location redeemed by God's intervention.

wordStudies.translatedAs: To wail, mourn, or grieve
wordStudies.meaning: To wail, mourn, or grieve. Israel's twenty-year mourning after the Lord reflected genuine sorrow over their broken relationship with God, not mere regret over military defeat.

wordStudies.translatedAs: Plural of Ashtoreth, the Canaanite fertility godde
wordStudies.meaning: Plural of Ashtoreth, the Canaanite fertility goddess associated with war and sexuality. Worship of Ashtaroth involved sacred prostitution and other practices incompatible with Israelite faith.

verses.text: The men of Kiriath Jearim came, and took Yahweh’s ark, and brought it into Abinadab’s house on the hill, and consecrated Eleazar his son to keep Yahweh’s ark.
verses.explainer: The men of Kiriath-jearim retrieve the ark and bring it to the house of Abinadab on the hill. His son Eleazar is consecrated to guard it. The ark finds a semi-permanent home.

verses.text: From the day that the ark stayed in Kiriath Jearim, the time was long; for it was twenty years; and all the house of Israel lamented after Yahweh.
verses.explainer: A long time passes -- twenty years -- and all Israel laments after the Lord. The extended period of mourning suggests the nation slowly recognized its spiritual condition.

thenVsNow.then: Spiritual renewal often takes longer than we expect. Israel needed twenty years to move from defeat to genuine repentance. Institutional and cultural change happens slowly.
thenVsNow.now: Spiritual renewal often takes longer than we expect. Israel needed twenty years to move from defeat to genuine repentance. Institutional and cultural change happens slowly.

verses.text: Samuel spoke to all the house of Israel, saying, “If you are returning to Yahweh with all your heart, then put away the foreign gods and the Ashtaroth from among you, and direct your hearts to Yahweh, and serve him only; and he will deliver you out of the hand of the Philistines.”
verses.explainer: Samuel tells all Israel that if they are returning to the Lord with all their hearts, they must put away foreign gods and Ashtaroth and direct their hearts to the Lord alone.

crossReferences.connection: Joshua issued the same call to put away foreign gods at the covenant renewal at Shechem. Samuel stands in the same prophetic tradition, calling each generation to renewed commitment.

verses.text: Then the children of Israel removed the Baals and the Ashtaroth, and served Yahweh only.
verses.explainer: The Israelites put away the Baals and Ashtaroth and serve the Lord only. This obedient response to Samuel's call represents genuine national repentance.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: Putting away idols was not merely a religious act but a cultural revolution. Baal worship was woven into agriculture, family life, and community identity. Abandoning it meant restructuring daily life around exclusive worship of the Lord.

verses.text: Samuel said, “Gather all Israel to Mizpah, and I will pray to Yahweh for you.”
verses.explainer: Samuel gathers all Israel to Mizpah, promising to pray for them. Mizpah becomes the site of a national assembly for prayer and intercession.

verses.text: They gathered together to Mizpah, and drew water, and poured it out before Yahweh, and fasted on that day, and said there, “We have sinned against Yahweh.” Samuel judged the children of Israel in Mizpah.
verses.explainer: The people draw water and pour it out before the Lord, fast, and confess their sins. Samuel judges Israel at Mizpah. The water pouring is a unique ritual of humility.

annotations.word: drew water, and poured it out

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: Fasting and confession were standard elements of repentance rituals in ancient Israel, but the water-pouring ceremony is found nowhere else in Scripture, suggesting a spontaneous expression of humility specific to this occasion.

verses.text: When the Philistines heard that the children of Israel were gathered together at Mizpah, the lords of the Philistines went up against Israel. When the children of Israel heard it, they were afraid of the Philistines.
verses.explainer: When the Philistines hear that Israel has gathered at Mizpah, they march against them. Israel's spiritual assembly is interrupted by military threat.

thenVsNow.then: The pattern of spiritual renewal being immediately tested by crisis is common in Scripture and in life. The Philistine attack came precisely when Israel was most vulnerable -- gathered unarmed for prayer, not war.
thenVsNow.now: The pattern of spiritual renewal being immediately tested by crisis is common in Scripture and in life. The Philistine attack came precisely when Israel was most vulnerable -- gathered unarmed for prayer, not war.

verses.text: The children of Israel said to Samuel, “Don’t stop crying to Yahweh our God for us, that he will save us out of the hand of the Philistines.”
verses.explainer: The Israelites ask Samuel not to stop crying out to the Lord for them. Their response to the threat is not to grab weapons but to ask for prayer -- a radical act of faith.

verses.text: Samuel took a suckling lamb, and offered it for a whole burnt offering to Yahweh. Samuel cried to Yahweh for Israel; and Yahweh answered him.
verses.explainer: Samuel offers a nursing lamb as a whole burnt offering and cries out to the Lord, and the Lord answers him. The sacrifice and prayer are genuine worship, not magical manipulation.

crossReferences.connection: In chapter 4, Israel brought the ark to battle as a lucky charm and lost. Here, they bring genuine prayer and sacrifice, and God responds with victory. The contrast is the lesson of the entire ark narrative.

verses.text: As Samuel was offering up the burnt offering, the Philistines came near to battle against Israel; but Yahweh thundered with a great thunder on that day on the Philistines, and confused them; and they were struck down before Israel.
verses.explainer: As Samuel offers the sacrifice, the Philistines attack. But the Lord thunders with a great thunder against them and throws them into confusion, and Israel defeats them.

annotations.word: Yahweh thundered with a great thunder

crossReferences.connection: God threw the Egyptian army into confusion at the Red Sea using the same divine warrior tactics. The pattern of God fighting for Israel when they trust him repeats from generation to generation.

verses.text: The men of Israel went out of Mizpah, and pursued the Philistines, and struck them, until they came under Beth Kar.
verses.explainer: The Israelites pursue the Philistines from Mizpah to Beth-car, striking them down along the way. The rout is complete.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: The pursuit from Mizpah downhill toward Beth-car took advantage of the terrain. The Philistines fled westward toward the coastal plain, with the Israelites chasing from the hill country -- a complete reversal of the typical Philistine advantage.

verses.text: Then Samuel took a stone, and set it between Mizpah and Shen, and called its name Ebenezer, saying, “Yahweh helped us until now.”
verses.explainer: Samuel sets up a stone between Mizpah and Shen and calls it Ebenezer, saying 'Thus far the Lord has helped us.' The memorial transforms a site of defeat into one of victory.

thenVsNow.then: Memorial stones and monuments served the same function as memorials today -- they anchored collective memory to specific events and places. Ebenezer reminds every future generation that God helped Israel when they returned to him with genuine faith.
thenVsNow.now: Memorial stones and monuments served the same function as memorials today -- they anchored collective memory to specific events and places. Ebenezer reminds every future generation that God helped Israel when they returned to him with genuine faith.

verses.text: So the Philistines were subdued, and they stopped coming within the border of Israel. Yahweh’s hand was against the Philistines all the days of Samuel.
verses.explainer: The Philistines are subdued and do not invade Israel's territory again during Samuel's time. The hand of the Lord is against the Philistines all the days of Samuel.

verses.text: The cities which the Philistines had taken from Israel were restored to Israel, from Ekron even to Gath; and Israel recovered its border out of the hand of the Philistines. There was peace between Israel and the Amorites.
verses.explainer: The cities the Philistines had taken are restored to Israel, from Ekron to Gath. There is also peace between Israel and the Amorites.

crossReferences.connection: Ekron, the last Philistine city to suffer from the ark's presence, is now the boundary of Israel's restored territory. The very cities that rejected the ark are now subject to Israel.

verses.text: Samuel judged Israel all the days of his life.
verses.explainer: Samuel judges Israel all the days of his life. His leadership spans the entire transition from judges to monarchy.

thenVsNow.then: Samuel's lifelong service as judge represents stable, faithful leadership -- a rarity in the book of Judges. His consistency made the people's later demand for a king all the more striking and ungrateful.
thenVsNow.now: Samuel's lifelong service as judge represents stable, faithful leadership -- a rarity in the book of Judges. His consistency made the people's later demand for a king all the more striking and ungrateful.

verses.text: He went from year to year in a circuit to Bethel, Gilgal, and Mizpah; and he judged Israel in all those places.
verses.explainer: Samuel goes on an annual circuit to Bethel, Gilgal, and Mizpah, judging Israel at all these places. His itinerant ministry brings justice and prophetic guidance across the land.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: Samuel's judicial circuit established a pattern of regional governance that supplemented the tribal system. Unlike earlier judges who were primarily military leaders, Samuel's role was judicial and spiritual -- he brought God's law and prophetic word to the people.

verses.text: His return was to Ramah, for his house was there; and he judged Israel there; and he built an altar to Yahweh there.
verses.explainer: Samuel returns to Ramah, his hometown, where he judges Israel and builds an altar. Ramah becomes both his home and the center of prophetic activity in Israel.

explainer.summary: When Samuel grows old, Israel's elders reject his sons (who are corrupt like Eli's) and demand a king 'like all the nations.' God tells Samuel that the people have not rejected him but God himself as their king. Samuel warns them in detail about the costs of monarchy -- conscription, taxation, confiscation of property -- but the people insist. God tells Samuel to give them what they want.
themes: The desire to be like other nations
God's permissive will versus his ideal will
explainer.contextInBook: Chapter 8 is the political crisis of 1 Samuel. Israel's demand for a king is presented as both a practical response to real problems (corrupt judges, Philistine threat) and a spiritual rejection of God's kingship. The tension between these two dimensions drives the rest of the book.

wordStudies.translatedAs: A sovereign ruler
wordStudies.meaning: A sovereign ruler. Israel's demand for a melek 'like all the nations' represented a fundamental shift from theocracy (God as king through judges and prophets) to monarchy (a human king with dynastic succession). The request itself was not sinful -- Deuteronomy 17:14-20 anticipated it -- but the motivation ('like all the nations') was.

wordStudies.translatedAs: To reject, refuse, or despise
wordStudies.meaning: To reject, refuse, or despise. God tells Samuel that the people have 'rejected' him as king. This strong verb conveys not mere preference but active repudiation of God's rule.

wordStudies.translatedAs: The 'manner' or 'custom' of the king (mishpat ha-m
wordStudies.meaning: The 'manner' or 'custom' of the king (mishpat ha-melek) describes the practices kings typically follow. Samuel's warning describes what kings do, not what they should do -- it is a realistic portrait of monarchic power.

verses.text: When Samuel was old, he made his sons judges over Israel.
verses.explainer: When Samuel is old, he appoints his sons as judges over Israel. The pattern of hereditary leadership continues despite its previous failures.

verses.text: Now the name of his firstborn was Joel; and the name of his second, Abijah. They were judges in Beersheba.
verses.explainer: His firstborn son Joel and his second son Abijah serve as judges at Beersheba, in the far south of Israel.

verses.text: His sons didn’t walk in his ways, but turned away after dishonest gain, took bribes, and perverted justice.
verses.explainer: Samuel's sons do not walk in his ways but turn aside after dishonest gain, take bribes, and pervert justice. History repeats: like Eli's sons, Samuel's sons corrupt their sacred office.

crossReferences.connection: The law explicitly forbids judges from taking bribes or perverting justice. Samuel's sons broke the fundamental commandments of the judicial office their father held with such integrity.

thenVsNow.then: The failure of leaders' children to live up to their parents' example is a recurring pattern in Scripture and in life. Integrity is not genetically inherited; each generation must choose it for themselves.
thenVsNow.now: The failure of leaders' children to live up to their parents' example is a recurring pattern in Scripture and in life. Integrity is not genetically inherited; each generation must choose it for themselves.

verses.text: Then all the elders of Israel gathered themselves together and came to Samuel to Ramah.
verses.explainer: All the elders of Israel gather and come to Samuel at Ramah. This is an official delegation representing the nation's leadership.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: The elders of Israel were the heads of the major families and clans who served as the nation's decision-making body. Their collective approach to Samuel gives the request for a king the weight of a national movement, not just a few discontented voices.

verses.text: They said to him, “Behold, you are old, and your sons don’t walk in your ways. Now make us a king to judge us like all the nations.”
verses.explainer: The elders point out that Samuel is old and his sons are corrupt, then make their demand: 'Now make us a king to judge us like all the nations.' The three-part argument is practical but the motivation is problematic.

crossReferences.connection: Moses anticipated that Israel would one day request a king and provided guidelines. The request itself was not wrong, but the motivation to be 'like all the nations' violated the spirit of Israel's calling.

verses.text:
But the thing displeased Samuel, when they said, “Give us a king to judge us.”
Samuel prayed to Yahweh.
verses.explainer: The request displeases Samuel, especially the phrase 'give us a king to judge us.' Samuel prays to the Lord, seeking divine guidance rather than reacting from personal offense.

verses.text: Yahweh said to Samuel, “Listen to the voice of the people in all that they tell you; for they have not rejected you, but they have rejected me as the king over them.
verses.explainer: God tells Samuel to listen to the people, because they have not rejected Samuel but God himself as their king. The political request is reframed as a theological crisis.

thenVsNow.then: God's willingness to allow something he disapproves of illustrates a profound principle: God sometimes gives people what they demand, even when it is not his ideal, allowing them to experience the consequences of their choices.
thenVsNow.now: God's willingness to allow something he disapproves of illustrates a profound principle: God sometimes gives people what they demand, even when it is not his ideal, allowing them to experience the consequences of their choices.

verses.text: According to all the works which they have done since the day that I brought them up out of Egypt even to this day, in that they have forsaken me, and served other gods, so they also do to you.
verses.explainer: God compares the current rejection to Israel's pattern since the exodus -- forsaking God and serving other gods. The request for a king fits a long history of unfaithfulness.

crossReferences.connection: The cyclical pattern of the book of Judges -- Israel turns from God, suffers, cries out, is delivered, and turns away again -- is the backdrop for this latest rejection.

verses.text: Now therefore listen to their voice. However you shall protest solemnly to them, and shall show them the way of the king who will reign over them.”
verses.explainer: God instructs Samuel to warn the people solemnly about the 'manner of the king' who will reign over them. They should know the cost before they choose.

verses.text: Samuel told all Yahweh’s words to the people who asked him for a king.
verses.explainer: Samuel tells the people all the words of the Lord. He faithfully communicates God's warning even though the people's decision will affect his own legacy.

thenVsNow.then: Samuel models what every faithful leader must do: give people honest information and let them choose, rather than manipulating outcomes. Respect for free choice, even when you disagree with the choice, is a mark of integrity.
thenVsNow.now: Samuel models what every faithful leader must do: give people honest information and let them choose, rather than manipulating outcomes. Respect for free choice, even when you disagree with the choice, is a mark of integrity.

verses.text: He said, “This will be the way of the king who shall reign over you: he will take your sons, and appoint them as his servants, for his chariots, and to be his horsemen; and they will run before his chariots.
verses.explainer: Samuel describes the king's practices: he will take their sons for his chariots and horsemen, and they will run before his chariots. Military conscription will be the first cost.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: Chariot forces were the ancient equivalent of a modern military's tanks and jets -- expensive, prestigious, and requiring enormous manpower and resources. A standing army with chariots would transform Israel from a volunteer militia to a conscription-based military state.

verses.text: He will appoint them to him for captains of thousands, and captains of fifties; and he will assign some to plow his ground, and to reap his harvest, and to make his instruments of war, and the instruments of his chariots.
verses.explainer: The king will appoint commanders of thousands and fifties, and make people plow his ground and reap his harvest and make weapons of war. Both military and agricultural labor will be conscripted.

thenVsNow.then: Forced labor for the state is a universal feature of centralized government. The tension between the benefits of organized government and the cost of individual freedom is one of the oldest political debates in human history, and Israel faces it here for the first time.
thenVsNow.now: Forced labor for the state is a universal feature of centralized government. The tension between the benefits of organized government and the cost of individual freedom is one of the oldest political debates in human history, and Israel faces it here for the first time.

verses.text: He will take your daughters to be perfumers, to be cooks, and to be bakers.
verses.explainer: The king will take their daughters to be perfumers, cooks, and bakers. Even women will be conscripted into royal service.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: Perfuming, cooking, and baking for the royal court were skilled positions but still represented forced service. In a culture where daughters were under their fathers' authority, the king's right to 'take' them represented a transfer of that authority to the state.

verses.text: He will take your fields, your vineyards, and your olive groves, even their best, and give them to his servants.
verses.explainer: The king will take the best fields, vineyards, and olive groves and give them to his servants. Property confiscation will fund royal patronage.

verses.text: He will take one tenth of your seed, and of your vineyards, and give it to his officers, and to his servants.
verses.explainer: The king will take a tenth of their grain and vintage and give it to his officers and servants. Royal taxation will parallel (and add to) the tithe already owed to the Lord.

crossReferences.connection: Solomon's heavy taxation and forced labor become the exact grievance that splits the kingdom after his death, fulfilling Samuel's warning precisely.

verses.text: He will take your male servants, your female servants, your best young men, and your donkeys, and assign them to his own work.
verses.explainer: The king will take their male and female servants, their best young men, and their donkeys, and put them to his work. Every resource -- human, animal, material -- is subject to royal conscription.

thenVsNow.then: Samuel's warning reads like a description of every overreaching government in history. The tendency of centralized power to consume the resources of the people it governs is not unique to ancient monarchy.
thenVsNow.now: Samuel's warning reads like a description of every overreaching government in history. The tendency of centralized power to consume the resources of the people it governs is not unique to ancient monarchy.

verses.text: He will take one tenth of your flocks; and you will be his servants.
verses.explainer: The king will take a tenth of their flocks, and the people themselves will become his servants. Samuel's final word is the most devastating: you will be slaves to the king you demanded.

verses.text: You will cry out in that day because of your king whom you will have chosen for yourselves; and Yahweh will not answer you in that day.”
verses.explainer: On that day they will cry out because of the king they chose, but the Lord will not answer. Having rejected God's rule, they will find no divine rescue from the consequences of their choice.

verses.text: But the people refused to listen to the voice of Samuel; and they said, “No; but we will have a king over us,
verses.explainer: The people refuse to listen to Samuel and insist: 'No! We will have a king over us!' Their rejection of the warning is deliberate and emphatic.

thenVsNow.then: The stubbornness of the people after being clearly warned is a universal human trait. We often choose what we want over what is wise, especially when desire is fueled by comparison with others.
thenVsNow.now: The stubbornness of the people after being clearly warned is a universal human trait. We often choose what we want over what is wise, especially when desire is fueled by comparison with others.

verses.text: that we also may be like all the nations, and that our king may judge us, and go out before us, and fight our battles.”
verses.explainer: They want to be like all the nations, with a king to judge them, go out before them, and fight their battles. They want a visible, human leader rather than an invisible God.

verses.text: Samuel heard all the words of the people, and he rehearsed them in the ears of Yahweh.
verses.explainer: Samuel hears all the people's words and reports them to the Lord. He continues to serve as mediator between the people and God even when they reject his counsel.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: Samuel's role as mediator -- hearing the people's demand and presenting it to God -- mirrors Moses at Sinai. The prophet stands between God and the nation, even when the nation's demand grieves him.

verses.text:
Yahweh said to Samuel, “Listen to their voice, and make them a king.”
Samuel said to the men of Israel, “Everyone go to your own city.”
verses.explainer: The Lord tells Samuel to listen to them and make them a king. Samuel sends the people home. God grants the request, setting the stage for Saul's selection.

crossReferences.connection: 'He gave them their request, but sent leanness into their soul.' The psalmist reflects on the pattern of God granting requests that ultimately impoverish the askers.

explainer.summary: Saul, a tall and handsome young Benjaminite, goes looking for his father's lost donkeys and instead finds the prophet Samuel. God has already told Samuel that he will send the future king of Israel the next day. Samuel hosts Saul at a feast, gives him the seat of honor, and privately begins to reveal God's plans for him.
themes: The contrast between human and divine criteria for kingship
explainer.contextInBook: Chapter 9 shifts from the public demand for a king (ch. 8) to God's private preparation of the man he has chosen. Saul's selection begins with mundane donkey-hunting, showing how God weaves his purposes through the fabric of everyday life.

wordStudies.translatedAs: One who sees -- an older term for prophet (navi)
wordStudies.meaning: One who sees -- an older term for prophet (navi). The seer perceived divine messages through visions and revelations. The narrator notes this older terminology to help later readers understand the story's setting.

wordStudies.translatedAs: An allotted share of food or inheritance
wordStudies.meaning: An allotted share of food or inheritance. The special portion Samuel reserved for Saul at the feast symbolically marked him as the chosen one before any public announcement.

verses.text: Now there was a man of Benjamin, whose name was Kish, the son of Abiel, the son of Zeror, the son of Becorath, the son of Aphiah, the son of a Benjamite, a mighty man of valor.
verses.explainer: Kish, a Benjaminite, is introduced as a man of wealth and standing. His son Saul is described as a choice young man -- tall, handsome, and impressive. The human qualifications for kingship are front and center.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: Physical appearance and stature were considered important qualifications for leadership in the ancient Near East. Kings were expected to look the part. Saul's impressive appearance meets human expectations perfectly.

verses.text: He had a son, whose name was Saul, an impressive young man; and there was not among the children of Israel a better person than he. From his shoulders and upward he was taller than any of the people.
verses.explainer: Saul is described as taller than anyone else in Israel, standing head and shoulders above the rest. His physical appearance will be contrasted later with David, whom God chooses by different criteria.

crossReferences.connection: God tells Samuel, 'Do not look on his appearance or his height... the Lord looks on the heart.' This directly contrasts the criteria that made Saul impressive with God's true standard for leadership.

verses.text: The donkeys of Kish, Saul’s father, were lost. Kish said to Saul his son, “Take now one of the servants with you, and arise, go look for the donkeys.”
verses.explainer: Kish's donkeys go missing, and he sends Saul with a servant to find them. God uses a mundane livestock problem to orchestrate the future king's meeting with the prophet.

thenVsNow.then: God's providence often works through the most ordinary circumstances. A lost donkey search becomes the vehicle for a divine appointment. The sacred and the mundane are not separate categories in God's economy.
thenVsNow.now: God's providence often works through the most ordinary circumstances. A lost donkey search becomes the vehicle for a divine appointment. The sacred and the mundane are not separate categories in God's economy.

verses.text: He passed through the hill country of Ephraim, and passed through the land of Shalishah, but they didn’t find them. Then they passed through the land of Shaalim, and there they weren’t there. Then he passed through the land of the Benjamites, but they didn’t find them.
verses.explainer: Saul searches through the hill country of Ephraim, the land of Shalishah, the land of Shaalim, and the land of Benjamin without finding the donkeys. The extended, fruitless search moves him toward God's destination.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: The search route traces a wide circuit through central Israel. Each failed stop moves Saul closer to Ramah, where Samuel lives. What feels like aimless wandering is divine direction.

verses.text: When they had come to the land of Zuph, Saul said to his servant who was with him, “Come, and let us return, lest my father stop caring about the donkeys, and be anxious for us.”
verses.explainer: When they reach the land of Zuph, Saul suggests returning home so his father will worry about them instead of the donkeys. Saul shows consideration for his father.

verses.text: The servant said to him, “Behold now, there is in this city a man of God, and he is a man who is held in honor. All that he says surely happens. Now let us go there. Perhaps he can tell us which way to go.”
verses.explainer: The servant suggests visiting the 'man of God' in the nearby city, who is held in honor and whose words always come true. The servant knows about Samuel; Saul apparently does not.

verses.text: Then Saul said to his servant, “But, behold, if we go, what should we bring the man? For the bread is spent in our sacks, and there is not a present to bring to the man of God. What do we have?”
verses.explainer: Saul worries they have nothing to bring the man of God -- their bread is gone. Bringing a gift to a prophet was customary, and showing up empty-handed was socially awkward.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: In the ancient Near East, you did not consult a religious figure without bringing a gift. This was not payment for services but a sign of respect. Saul's concern shows proper social awareness.

verses.text: The servant answered Saul again, and said, “Behold, I have in my hand the fourth part of a shekel of silver. I will give that to the man of God, to tell us our way.”
verses.explainer: The servant has a quarter shekel of silver and offers to give it to the man of God. A servant solves what the future king cannot -- another subtle hint about Saul's dependence on others for direction.

verses.text: (In earlier times in Israel, when a man went to inquire of God, he said, “Come, and let us go to the seer”; for he who is now called a prophet was before called a seer.)
verses.explainer: The narrator inserts a parenthetical note explaining that a prophet used to be called a 'seer.' This editorial comment tells us the story was written down long after the events occurred.

verses.text: Then Saul said to his servant, “Well said. Come, let us go.” So they went to the city where the man of God was.
verses.explainer: Saul agrees to go see the man of God. They head toward the city where Samuel lives.

thenVsNow.then: Saul's willingness to seek a prophet's help, even over something as mundane as lost donkeys, reflects a worldview in which spiritual guidance applied to all of life, not just 'religious' matters.
thenVsNow.now: Saul's willingness to seek a prophet's help, even over something as mundane as lost donkeys, reflects a worldview in which spiritual guidance applied to all of life, not just 'religious' matters.

verses.text: As they went up the ascent to the city, they found young maidens going out to draw water, and said to them, “Is the seer here?”
verses.explainer: Going up the hill to the city, they meet young women coming out to draw water and ask if the seer is there. The women provide directions.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: Drawing water was a daily task for young women, and the well or spring was a natural place to gather information. Travelers routinely asked locals for directions at these sites.

verses.text: They answered them, and said, “He is. Behold, he is before you. Hurry now, for he has come today into the city; for the people have a sacrifice today in the high place.
verses.explainer: The women say the seer arrived just today because there is a sacrifice at the high place. They urge Saul to hurry before the feast begins.

verses.text: As soon as you have come into the city, you will immediately find him, before he goes up to the high place to eat; for the people will not eat until he come, because he blesses the sacrifice. Afterwards those who are invited eat. Now therefore go up; for at this time you will find him.”
verses.explainer: The people will not eat until the seer arrives to bless the sacrifice. Samuel's role as spiritual leader means the entire community waits for his priestly function before the meal can begin.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: The blessing of the sacrifice by a prophetic or priestly figure was essential before a communal meal could proceed. This reflects the deep integration of religion and daily life in ancient Israel.

verses.text: They went up to the city. As they came within the city, behold, Samuel came out toward them, to go up to the high place.
verses.explainer: Saul and his servant go up to the city, and as they enter, Samuel comes out toward them on his way to the high place. The timing is precisely orchestrated by God.

thenVsNow.then: The 'chance' meeting between Saul and Samuel is anything but chance. God's timing arranged for their paths to cross at exactly the right moment -- a reminder that what we call coincidence may be Providence at work.
thenVsNow.now: The 'chance' meeting between Saul and Samuel is anything but chance. God's timing arranged for their paths to cross at exactly the right moment -- a reminder that what we call coincidence may be Providence at work.

verses.text: Now Yahweh had revealed to Samuel a day before Saul came, saying,
verses.explainer: The day before, the Lord had revealed to Samuel that he would send a man from Benjamin whom Samuel should anoint as prince over Israel to save them from the Philistines.

verses.text: “Tomorrow about this time I will send you a man out of the land of Benjamin, and you shall anoint him to be prince over my people Israel. He will save my people out of the hand of the Philistines; for I have looked upon my people, because their cry has come to me.”
verses.explainer: God tells Samuel to anoint this man as prince (nagid) over Israel. God has seen his people's affliction and heard their cry -- language echoing the exodus.

crossReferences.connection: God's statement 'I have seen the affliction of my people and heard their cry' directly echoes his words to Moses at the burning bush, framing Saul's anointing as a new deliverance.

verses.text: When Samuel saw Saul, Yahweh said to him, “Behold, the man of whom I spoke to you! He will have authority over my people.”
verses.explainer: When Samuel sees Saul, the Lord tells him, 'Here is the man I told you about; he shall reign over my people.' God's direct identification removes any doubt.

verses.text: Then Saul approached Samuel in the gateway, and said, “Please tell me where the seer’s house is.”
verses.explainer: Saul approaches Samuel in the gateway and asks where the seer's house is. He does not know he is speaking to the very person he seeks.

thenVsNow.then: The irony of Saul asking Samuel where to find Samuel captures a spiritual truth: sometimes we are searching for something that is already standing right in front of us.
thenVsNow.now: The irony of Saul asking Samuel where to find Samuel captures a spiritual truth: sometimes we are searching for something that is already standing right in front of us.

verses.text: Samuel answered Saul, and said, “I am the seer. Go up before me to the high place, for you are to eat with me today. In the morning I will let you go, and will tell you all that is in your heart.
verses.explainer: Samuel identifies himself as the seer and invites Saul to eat with him. He tells Saul not to worry about the donkeys -- they have been found. Samuel also hints that Saul is the focus of all Israel's desire.

verses.text: As for your donkeys who were lost three days ago, don’t set your mind on them; for they have been found. For whom is all that is desirable in Israel? Is it not for you, and for all your father’s house?”
verses.explainer: Samuel tells Saul the donkeys that went missing three days ago have been found. Then he adds the stunning question: for whom is all that is desirable in Israel? Is it not for you and your father's house?

thenVsNow.then: Saul came looking for donkeys and found a kingdom. The gap between what we seek and what God intends to give us is sometimes vast beyond imagination.
thenVsNow.now: Saul came looking for donkeys and found a kingdom. The gap between what we seek and what God intends to give us is sometimes vast beyond imagination.

verses.text: Saul answered, “Am I not a Benjamite, of the smallest of the tribes of Israel? And my family the least of all the families of the tribe of Benjamin? Why then do you speak to me like this?”
verses.explainer: Saul responds with genuine humility: he is from Benjamin, the smallest tribe, and his family is the least of all Benjamin's families. Why would Samuel say such things to him?

crossReferences.connection: Gideon also protested, 'My clan is the weakest in Manasseh, and I am the least in my father's house.' God's pattern of choosing the small and unlikely is consistent.

verses.text: Samuel took Saul and his servant, and brought them into the guest room, and made them sit in the best place among those who were invited, who were about thirty persons.
verses.explainer: Samuel brings Saul and his servant into the hall, seats them at the head of the invited guests -- about thirty people. The seat of honor publicly marks Saul as special before anyone knows why.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: Seating at an ancient Near Eastern banquet was highly significant. The head of the table was the place of greatest honor. Samuel's public elevation of this unknown traveler would have been immediately noticed by the other guests.

verses.text: Samuel said to the cook, “Bring the portion which I gave you, of which I said to you, ‘Set it aside.’”
verses.explainer: Samuel tells the cook to bring the special portion he set aside. The reserved portion was prepared in advance for the man God told Samuel was coming.

verses.text: The cook took up the thigh, and that which was on it, and set it before Saul. Samuel said, “Behold, that which has been reserved! Set it before yourself and eat; because for the appointed time has it been kept for you, for I said, ‘I have invited the people.’” So Saul ate with Samuel that day.
verses.explainer: The cook sets before Saul the leg and what was on it -- the choice portion that had been reserved. Samuel explains that it was kept for this appointed time. Saul eats with Samuel that day.

verses.text: When they had come down from the high place into the city, he talked with Saul on the housetop.
verses.explainer: After coming down from the high place, Samuel speaks with Saul on the roof of his house. The private rooftop conversation begins the disclosure of Saul's destiny.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: Flat rooftops in ancient Israel served as private spaces for conversation, prayer, and sleeping in warm weather. A rooftop conversation was intimate and confidential -- the perfect setting for Samuel to reveal God's plans.

verses.text: They arose early; and about daybreak, Samuel called to Saul on the housetop, saying, “Get up, that I may send you away.” Saul arose, and they both went outside, he and Samuel, together.
verses.explainer: At dawn Samuel calls to Saul on the roof, telling him to get up so he can send him on his way. Samuel will now escort him out of the city for a private moment.

thenVsNow.then: The dawn departure after a night of rooftop conversation suggests Samuel spent hours preparing Saul for what was coming. Significant spiritual transitions often require extended, unhurried preparation.
thenVsNow.now: The dawn departure after a night of rooftop conversation suggests Samuel spent hours preparing Saul for what was coming. Significant spiritual transitions often require extended, unhurried preparation.

verses.text: As they were going down at the end of the city, Samuel said to Saul, “Tell the servant to go on ahead of us.” He went ahead, then Samuel said, “But stand still first, that I may cause you to hear God’s message.”
verses.explainer: As they reach the edge of the city, Samuel tells Saul to send his servant ahead so that Samuel can share God's word with him privately. The anointing will be secret.

explainer.summary: Samuel privately anoints Saul as king and gives him three confirming signs that all come true. The Spirit of God rushes upon Saul and he prophesies among the prophets, prompting the saying 'Is Saul also among the prophets?' At a public assembly at Mizpah, Saul is chosen by lot but hides among the baggage. He is brought forward, standing head and shoulders above everyone, and is presented as king.
themes: Anointing and the Spirit of God
The tension between divine choice and public selection
explainer.contextInBook: Chapter 10 bridges the private anointing and the public presentation of Saul. The three signs validate God's choice, and the Spirit's coming equips Saul for leadership. But his hiding among the baggage foreshadows the insecurity that will eventually destroy him.

wordStudies.translatedAs: To smear or pour oil on someone, consecrating them
wordStudies.meaning: To smear or pour oil on someone, consecrating them for a sacred role. Anointing with oil symbolized the Holy Spirit's empowerment and God's choice. Kings, priests, and sometimes prophets were anointed.

wordStudies.translatedAs: The reflexive form of the verb 'to prophesy,' sugg
wordStudies.meaning: The reflexive form of the verb 'to prophesy,' suggesting ecstatic prophetic behavior -- being seized by the Spirit and speaking or acting under divine influence. This was distinct from the deliberate prophecy of figures like Samuel.

wordStudies.translatedAs: Lots were objects (stones, sticks, or tokens) used
wordStudies.meaning: Lots were objects (stones, sticks, or tokens) used to determine God's will. The process was considered a reliable way to discern divine choice, since God controlled the outcome (Proverbs 16:33).

verses.text: Then Samuel took the vial of oil, and poured it on his head, and kissed him, and said, “Hasn’t Yahweh anointed you to be prince over his inheritance?
verses.explainer: Samuel takes a flask of oil and pours it on Saul's head, kisses him, and declares that the Lord has anointed him as prince over his inheritance. The private ceremony is intimate and solemn.

crossReferences.connection: David's later anointing by Samuel parallels this scene but with a crucial difference: the Spirit comes upon David permanently, while Saul's anointing will ultimately be revoked.

verses.text: When you have departed from me today, then you will find two men by Rachel’s tomb, on the border of Benjamin at Zelzah. They will tell you, ‘The donkeys which you went to look for have been found; and behold, your father has stopped caring about the donkeys, and is anxious for you, saying, “What shall I do for my son?”’
verses.explainer: Samuel gives the first sign: at Rachel's tomb near Zelzah, two men will tell Saul the donkeys are found and his father now worries about him. The mundane concern that started the journey is resolved.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: Rachel's tomb was a well-known landmark in Benjamin. Using it as a meeting point grounded the prophetic sign in geography every Israelite would recognize.

verses.text: “Then you will go on forward from there, and you will come to the oak of Tabor. Three men will meet you there going up to God to Bethel, one carrying three young goats, and another carrying three loaves of bread, and another carrying a bottle of wine.
verses.explainer: The second sign: at the oak of Tabor, three men going up to God at Bethel will meet Saul -- one carrying three young goats, one three loaves of bread, and one a skin of wine.

verses.text: They will greet you, and give you two loaves of bread, which you shall receive from their hand.
verses.explainer: The men will greet Saul and give him two loaves of bread, which he is to accept. Accepting provisions from pilgrims headed to worship marks Saul as someone worthy of honor in Israel's religious community.

thenVsNow.then: The signs Samuel gives are not spectacular miracles but highly specific ordinary events. God's confirmation often comes not through the miraculous but through the precise arrangement of everyday circumstances.
thenVsNow.now: The signs Samuel gives are not spectacular miracles but highly specific ordinary events. God's confirmation often comes not through the miraculous but through the precise arrangement of everyday circumstances.

verses.text: “After that you will come to the hill of God, where the garrison of the Philistines is; and it will happen, when you have come there to the city, that you will meet a band of prophets coming down from the high place with a lute, a tambourine, a pipe, and a harp before them; and they will be prophesying.
verses.explainer: The third sign: at the hill of God (where there is a Philistine garrison), Saul will meet a band of prophets coming down from the high place, prophesying with musical instruments. The Spirit will rush upon him.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: Prophetic bands or guilds were communities of prophets who worshiped and prophesied together, often with musical accompaniment (harps, tambourines, flutes, lyres). Music was understood to facilitate prophetic experience.

verses.text: Then Yahweh’s Spirit will come mightily on you, and you will prophesy with them, and will be turned into another man.
verses.explainer: The Spirit of the Lord will rush upon Saul, and he will prophesy with the prophets and be turned into another man. The Spirit transforms Saul's very identity.

crossReferences.connection: The Spirit 'rushing upon' someone is the same language used for Samson. The Spirit empowers for specific tasks, though in Saul's case this empowerment will eventually be withdrawn.

verses.text: Let it be, when these signs have come to you, that you do what is appropriate for the occasion; for God is with you.
verses.explainer: Samuel tells Saul that when these signs come to pass, he should do whatever his hand finds to do, for God is with him. This is a commission to act boldly in God's strength.

verses.text: “Go down ahead of me to Gilgal; and behold, I will come down to you, to offer burnt offerings, and to sacrifice sacrifices of peace offerings. Wait seven days, until I come to you, and show you what you are to do.”
verses.explainer: Samuel instructs Saul to go to Gilgal and wait seven days until Samuel arrives to offer sacrifices. This specific instruction will become the test that Saul fails in chapter 13.

crossReferences.connection: Saul's failure to wait for Samuel at Gilgal as instructed here becomes the occasion for the first rejection of his dynasty. This verse sets up one of the book's most pivotal moments.

verses.text: It was so, that when he had turned his back to go from Samuel, God gave him another heart; and all those signs happened that day.
verses.explainer: When Saul turns to leave Samuel, God gives him another heart, and all the signs come true that day. The transformation is immediate and the signs are precisely fulfilled.

verses.text: When they came there to the hill, behold, a band of prophets met him; and the Spirit of God came mightily on him, and he prophesied among them.
verses.explainer: When Saul arrives at the hill, a band of prophets meets him, the Spirit of God rushes upon him, and he prophesies among them. The third sign is spectacularly fulfilled.

thenVsNow.then: The experience of God's Spirit seizing someone and producing unexpected behavior has parallels across Christian history -- from Pentecost to revival movements. The Spirit's work is unpredictable and transformative.
thenVsNow.now: The experience of God's Spirit seizing someone and producing unexpected behavior has parallels across Christian history -- from Pentecost to revival movements. The Spirit's work is unpredictable and transformative.

verses.text: When all who knew him before saw that, behold, he prophesied with the prophets, then the people said to one another, “What is this that has come to the son of Kish? Is Saul also among the prophets?”
verses.explainer: Those who knew Saul before are astonished: 'Is Saul also among the prophets?' The proverb arises from the shock of seeing this ordinary Benjaminite in ecstatic prophetic worship.

annotations.word: Is Saul also among the prophets?

verses.text: One of the same place answered, “Who is their father?” Therefore it became a proverb, “Is Saul also among the prophets?”
verses.explainer: A local man adds, 'And who is their father?' The question implies that prophetic authority comes from God, not from family lineage -- any person can be seized by the Spirit.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: The question 'who is their father?' may mean prophets have no human pedigree that explains their gift -- they are all equally surprised by God's Spirit. Alternatively, it asks who the leader of this prophetic band is, implying Saul could lead even prophets.

verses.text: When he had finished prophesying, he came to the high place.
verses.explainer: When Saul finishes prophesying, he goes to the high place. The ecstatic experience ends and normal life resumes.

thenVsNow.then: Powerful spiritual experiences are temporary -- they equip and confirm but do not replace the daily choices of faithfulness. Saul's prophetic experience empowered him for kingship but did not guarantee his obedience.
thenVsNow.now: Powerful spiritual experiences are temporary -- they equip and confirm but do not replace the daily choices of faithfulness. Saul's prophetic experience empowered him for kingship but did not guarantee his obedience.

verses.text:
Saul’s uncle said to him and to his servant, “Where did you go?”
He said, “To seek the donkeys. When we saw that they were not found, we came to Samuel.”
verses.explainer: Saul's uncle asks where they went. Saul tells him they went to see Samuel when the donkeys were not found. He does not mention the anointing.

verses.text: Saul’s uncle said, “Please tell me what Samuel said to you.”
verses.explainer: The uncle presses: what did Samuel say? Saul reveals only that Samuel said the donkeys were found. He conceals the matter of the kingship.

thenVsNow.then: Saul's discretion here is admirable -- he does not boast about his anointing or seek to elevate himself prematurely. This restraint is a positive trait that will unfortunately not last.
thenVsNow.now: Saul's discretion here is admirable -- he does not boast about his anointing or seek to elevate himself prematurely. This restraint is a positive trait that will unfortunately not last.

verses.text: Saul said to his uncle, “He told us plainly that the donkeys were found.” But concerning the matter of the kingdom, of which Samuel spoke, he didn’t tell him.
verses.explainer: Saul does not tell his uncle about the kingship. The secret anointing remains private, waiting for the public confirmation.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: Keeping the anointing secret was both strategically wise and spiritually appropriate. Public announcement required the proper setting and authority -- Samuel would orchestrate the public selection in God's timing.

verses.text: Samuel called the people together to Yahweh to Mizpah;
verses.explainer: Samuel summons the people to the Lord at Mizpah for the public selection of the king. The same location where Israel gathered for repentance in chapter 7 now hosts the coronation.

crossReferences.connection: Mizpah was the site of Israel's great repentance and victory over the Philistines. Choosing it for the king selection connects the new institution to that moment of spiritual renewal.

verses.text: and he said to the children of Israel, “Yahweh, the God of Israel, says ‘I brought Israel up out of Egypt, and I delivered you out of the hand of the Egyptians, and out of the hand of all the kingdoms that oppressed you.’
verses.explainer: Samuel delivers God's message to Israel, reminding them that God brought them out of Egypt and delivered them from all the kingdoms that oppressed them.

annotations.word: I brought Israel up out of Egypt

verses.text: But you have today rejected your God, who himself saves you out of all your calamities and your distresses; and you have said to him, ‘No! Set a king over us.’ Now therefore present yourselves before Yahweh by your tribes, and by your thousands.”
verses.explainer: God accuses Israel of rejecting the one who saves them from all their calamities and distresses by demanding a king. He instructs them to present themselves by tribes.

thenVsNow.then: God grants the people's request while simultaneously pointing out its foolishness. This pattern -- giving people what they ask for while making clear it was not the best option -- characterizes divine patience throughout Scripture.
thenVsNow.now: God grants the people's request while simultaneously pointing out its foolishness. This pattern -- giving people what they ask for while making clear it was not the best option -- characterizes divine patience throughout Scripture.

verses.text: So Samuel brought all the tribes of Israel near, and the tribe of Benjamin was chosen.
verses.explainer: Samuel brings the tribes near, and the lot falls on the tribe of Benjamin. The selection process narrows from tribe to clan to family to individual.

verses.text: He brought the tribe of Benjamin near by their families; and the family of the Matrites was chosen. Then Saul the son of Kish was chosen; but when they looked for him, he could not be found.
verses.explainer: The lot narrows to the family of Matri and then to Saul himself. But when they look for him, he cannot be found.

verses.text:
Therefore they asked of Yahweh further, “Is there yet a man to come here?”
Yahweh answered, “Behold, he has hidden himself among the baggage.”
verses.explainer: They inquire of the Lord, who reveals that Saul has hidden himself among the baggage. The future king is found hiding behind the luggage.

thenVsNow.then: Hiding from responsibility at the moment of calling is deeply human. Moses protested at the burning bush, Gideon questioned the angel, Jonah fled to Tarshish. Reluctance before a great calling is normal; the question is whether it gives way to courage.
thenVsNow.now: Hiding from responsibility at the moment of calling is deeply human. Moses protested at the burning bush, Gideon questioned the angel, Jonah fled to Tarshish. Reluctance before a great calling is normal; the question is whether it gives way to courage.

verses.text: They ran and got him there. When he stood among the people, he was higher than any of the people from his shoulders and upward.
verses.explainer: They run and bring Saul from among the baggage. When he stands among the people, he is taller than any of them from the shoulders up. His physical impressiveness is emphasized again.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: Saul's height fulfilled the visual expectations of kingship in the ancient Near East, where rulers were often depicted as physically larger than their subjects. His appearance was designed to impress.

verses.text:
Samuel said to all the people, “Do you see him whom Yahweh has chosen, that there is no one like him among all the people?”
All the people shouted, and said, “Long live the king!”
verses.explainer: Samuel presents Saul: 'Do you see him whom the Lord has chosen? There is none like him among all the people!' The people shout 'Long live the king!' Israel has its first monarch.
