verses.text: We made a raid on the South of the Cherethites, and on that which belongs to Judah, and on the South of Caleb; and we burned Ziklag with fire.”
verses.explainer: The Egyptian reveals that the Amalekites raided the Negev of the Cherethites, the territory of Judah, and the Negev of Caleb, and burned Ziklag.

verses.text:
David said to him, “Will you bring me down to this troop?”
He said, “Swear to me by God that you will not kill me and not deliver me up into the hands of my master, and I will bring you down to this troop.”
verses.explainer: David asks the Egyptian to lead them to the raiding party. The man agrees, on condition that David swears not to kill him or return him to his master. David swears.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: The Egyptian's conditions reveal the desperate position of slaves in the ancient world -- he fears death or re-enslavement more than anything. David's oath protects him and ensures his cooperation.

verses.text: When he had brought him down, behold, they were spread around over all the ground, eating, drinking, and dancing, because of all the great plunder that they had taken out of the land of the Philistines, and out of the land of Judah.
verses.explainer: The Egyptian leads them to the Amalekites, who are spread out eating, drinking, and celebrating their massive plunder from Philistia and Judah. They are completely unsuspecting.

verses.text: David struck them from the twilight even to the evening of the next day. Not a man of them escaped from there, except four hundred young men, who rode on camels and fled.
verses.explainer: David attacks them from twilight until the evening of the next day. No one escapes except four hundred young men who flee on camels.

verses.text: David recovered all that the Amalekites had taken; and David rescued his two wives.
verses.explainer: David recovers everything the Amalekites had taken. Nothing is missing -- small or great, sons or daughters, plunder or anything. David brings it all back.

verses.text: There was nothing lacking to them, neither small nor great, neither sons nor daughters, neither plunder, nor anything that they had taken to them. David brought back all.
verses.explainer: Nothing is missing -- young or old, sons or daughters, spoil or anything that had been taken. David brings it all back. The narrator emphasizes the totality of the recovery.

crossReferences.connection: God promised complete recovery, and complete recovery is what David receives. Faith in God's specific promises produces specific fulfillment.

verses.text: David took all the flocks and the herds, which they drove before those other livestock, and said, “This is David’s plunder.”
verses.explainer: David also takes all the Amalekite flocks and herds, which are driven ahead of his recovered livestock. People say, 'This is David's spoil.' He gains more than he lost.

thenVsNow.then: David returns from the battle with everything that was taken plus additional plunder. God's restoration exceeds the original loss -- a pattern of divine generosity that runs throughout Scripture.
thenVsNow.now: David returns from the battle with everything that was taken plus additional plunder. God's restoration exceeds the original loss -- a pattern of divine generosity that runs throughout Scripture.

verses.text: David came to the two hundred men, who were so faint that they could not follow David, whom also they had made to stay at the brook Besor; and they went out to meet David, and to meet the people who were with him. When David came near to the people, he greeted them.
verses.explainer: David returns to the two hundred men who stayed at the Brook Besor. They come out to meet David and the people, and David greets them peacefully.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: The reunion at the Brook Besor sets up the test case. The two hundred who stayed behind will want a share of the plunder. The four hundred who fought will object. David must adjudicate.

verses.text: Then all the wicked men and worthless fellows, of those who went with David, answered and said, “Because they didn’t go with us, we will not give them anything of the plunder that we have recovered, except to every man his wife and his children, that he may lead them away, and depart.”
verses.explainer: Worthless men among those who went with David say the two hundred who stayed should receive nothing except their own families. They did not fight, so they should not share.

verses.text: Then David said, “Do not do so, my brothers, with that which Yahweh has given to us, who has preserved us, and delivered the troop that came against us into our hand.
verses.explainer: David says no: God gave them the victory and protected them. What God has given, they will not hoard. He redirects credit from human effort to divine gift.

verses.text: Who will listen to you in this matter? For as his share is who goes down to the battle, so shall his share be who stays with the baggage. They shall share alike.”
verses.explainer: David establishes a rule: the share of those who go to battle and those who stay with the baggage will be equal. Those who guard and those who fight receive the same.

thenVsNow.then: David's principle of equal shares for combat and support troops anticipates modern military compensation policy. Everyone who contributes to the mission shares in the reward, regardless of their specific role.
thenVsNow.now: David's principle of equal shares for combat and support troops anticipates modern military compensation policy. Everyone who contributes to the mission shares in the reward, regardless of their specific role.

verses.text: It was so from that day forward, that he made it a statute and an ordinance for Israel to this day.
verses.explainer: From that day forward, David makes this a statute and ordinance for Israel 'to this day.' The editorial note confirms this became permanent law.

crossReferences.connection: Moses established a similar principle for dividing spoil between fighters and the community. David's ruling extends and formalizes this Mosaic precedent.

verses.text: When David came to Ziklag, he sent some of the plunder to the elders of Judah, even to his friends, saying, “Behold, a present for you from the plunder of Yahweh’s enemies.”
verses.explainer: When David comes to Ziklag, he sends some of the spoil to the elders of Judah, saying, 'Here is a present for you from the spoil of the enemies of the Lord.'

verses.text: He sent it to those who were in Bethel, to those who were in Ramoth of the South, to those who were in Jattir,
verses.explainer: David sends gifts to those in Bethel, Ramoth of the Negev, Jattir, and other cities where he and his men had traveled. He rewards those who sheltered him.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: The list of cities receiving gifts traces David's fugitive journey. He remembers and rewards the communities that helped him during his years as an outlaw. This gratitude-based distribution builds a network of loyal supporters.

verses.text: to those who were in Aroer, to those who were in Siphmoth, to those who were in Eshtemoa,
verses.explainer: Gifts go to Aroer, Siphmoth, Eshtemoa, and other cities in the Negev and Shephelah.

thenVsNow.then: David's gift-giving creates a web of loyalty across southern Judah. This is not merely generosity but strategic relationship-building. When the time comes for David to become king, these communities will support him.
thenVsNow.now: David's gift-giving creates a web of loyalty across southern Judah. This is not merely generosity but strategic relationship-building. When the time comes for David to become king, these communities will support him.

verses.text: to those who were in Racal, to those who were in the cities of the Jerahmeelites, to those who were in the cities of the Kenites,
verses.explainer: Gifts go to Rachal, the cities of the Jerahmeelites, the cities of the Kenites.

crossReferences.connection: The Jerahmeelites and Kenites were the peoples David claimed to be raiding when he lied to Achish. Now he sends them gifts, confirming he never actually attacked them.

verses.text: to those who were in Hormah, to those who were in Borashan, to those who were in Athach,
verses.explainer: Gifts go to Hormah, Bor-ashan, Athach, and Hebron -- all places where David and his men had roamed.

verses.text: to those who were in Hebron, and to all the places where David himself and his men used to stay.
verses.explainer: David distributes gifts to all the places where he and his men had traveled. The comprehensive list shows the breadth of David's network and influence across southern Judah.

thenVsNow.then: The chapter ends with David at the height of his influence in Judah. While Saul marches toward death at Gilboa, David consolidates support across the south. The contrast between their trajectories -- one ascending, one falling -- reaches its conclusion in the next chapter.
thenVsNow.now: The chapter ends with David at the height of his influence in Judah. While Saul marches toward death at Gilboa, David consolidates support across the south. The contrast between their trajectories -- one ascending, one falling -- reaches its conclusion in the next chapter.

explainer.summary: The Philistines defeat Israel at Mount Gilboa. Jonathan, Abinadab, and Malchi-shua -- Saul's three sons -- are killed. Saul is critically wounded by archers. Rather than be captured and tortured, he falls on his own sword. The Philistines find the bodies, strip Saul's armor, display it in the temple of the Ashtaroth, and fasten the bodies to the wall of Beth-shan. The men of Jabesh-gilead -- remembering Saul's rescue of their city -- travel through the night to recover the bodies and give them a proper burial.
themes: The tragic end of a gifted but disobedient king
explainer.contextInBook: Chapter 31 completes the narrative arc of 1 Samuel with devastating finality. Everything Samuel prophesied at Endor comes true. The king who began with such promise -- humble, Spirit-filled, victorious -- dies in disgrace by his own hand. But the chapter ends with an act of noble loyalty, hinting that even in Israel's darkest hour, faithfulness survives.

wordStudies.translatedAs: Suicide by falling on one's own weapon
wordStudies.meaning: Suicide by falling on one's own weapon. Saul's self-inflicted death was motivated by the desire to avoid the humiliation of Philistine capture, torture, and public display. Ancient Israelite texts neither explicitly condemn nor condone his act.

verses.text: Now the Philistines fought against Israel; and the men of Israel fled from before the Philistines, and fell down slain on Mount Gilboa.
verses.explainer: The Philistines fight against Israel. The Israelites flee before them, and many fall slain on Mount Gilboa. Samuel's prophecy is fulfilled.

crossReferences.connection: Samuel's ghost at Endor predicted: 'Tomorrow you and your sons will be with me... the Lord will give Israel into the hand of the Philistines.' Every word comes true.

verses.text: The Philistines overtook Saul and on his sons; and the Philistines killed Jonathan, Abinadab, and Malchishua, the sons of Saul.
verses.explainer: The Philistines press hard after Saul and kill his three sons: Jonathan, Abinadab, and Malchi-shua. The royal family is destroyed.

thenVsNow.then: Jonathan's death with his father rather than fleeing to David represents his final act of loyalty. He could have saved himself by joining David but chose to stand with his family. His faithfulness to his father, even a deeply flawed father, is its own form of honor.
thenVsNow.now: Jonathan's death with his father rather than fleeing to David represents his final act of loyalty. He could have saved himself by joining David but chose to stand with his family. His faithfulness to his father, even a deeply flawed father, is its own form of honor.

verses.text: The battle went hard against Saul, and the archers overtook him; and he was greatly distressed by reason of the archers.
verses.explainer: The battle goes heavily against Saul. The archers find him, and he is badly wounded. Ancient arrow wounds were often fatal, as arrows could not be safely extracted.

verses.text: Then Saul said to his armor bearer, “Draw your sword, and thrust me through with it, lest these uncircumcised come and thrust me through, and abuse me!” But his armor bearer would not; for he was terrified. Therefore Saul took his sword, and fell on it.
verses.explainer: Saul tells his armor-bearer to draw his sword and thrust it through him, so the uncircumcised Philistines do not abuse him. The armor-bearer refuses, terrified. Saul falls on his own sword.

thenVsNow.then: Saul's death is tragic precisely because it is consistent with his character. Fear of people drove his disobedience, his jealousy, and his persecution of David. In the end, fear of what the Philistines would do drives his suicide. He dies as he lived -- controlled by fear.
thenVsNow.now: Saul's death is tragic precisely because it is consistent with his character. Fear of people drove his disobedience, his jealousy, and his persecution of David. In the end, fear of what the Philistines would do drives his suicide. He dies as he lived -- controlled by fear.

verses.text: When his armor bearer saw that Saul was dead, he likewise fell on his sword, and died with him.
verses.explainer: When the armor-bearer sees that Saul is dead, he also falls on his sword and dies with him. Loyalty extends to death.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: The armor-bearer's suicide reflects the ancient warrior code: a personal attendant's life was bound to his master's. When the master fell, the attendant's purpose ended. His death, while tragic, was considered honorable in the ancient context.

verses.text: So Saul died, and his three sons, and his armor bearer, and all his men, that same day together.
verses.explainer: So Saul dies, his three sons, his armor-bearer, and all his men together that day. The summary is comprehensive and devastating.

annotations.word: all his men, that same day together

verses.text: When the men of Israel who were on the other side of the valley, and those who were beyond the Jordan, saw that the men of Israel fled, and that Saul and his sons were dead, they abandoned the cities and fled; and the Philistines came and lived in them.
verses.explainer: When the Israelites on the other side of the valley see the army has fled and Saul and his sons are dead, they abandon their cities and flee. The Philistines occupy them.

annotations.word: the Philistines came and lived in them

verses.text: On the next day, when the Philistines came to strip the slain, they found Saul and his three sons fallen on Mount Gilboa.
verses.explainer: The next day, the Philistines come to strip the dead and find Saul and his three sons fallen on Mount Gilboa.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: Stripping the dead of armor, weapons, and valuables was standard practice after battle. It was both economically motivated and a final act of domination over the defeated enemy.

verses.text: They cut off his head, stripped off his armor, and sent into the land of the Philistines all around, to carry the news to the house of their idols, and to the people.
verses.explainer: They cut off Saul's head and strip his armor. They send messengers throughout Philistia to carry the news to their temples and people.

crossReferences.connection: David cut off Goliath's head; now the Philistines cut off Saul's head. What Israel did to the Philistine champion, the Philistines do to Israel's king.

verses.text: They put his armor in the house of the Ashtaroth, and they fastened his body to the wall of Beth Shan.
verses.explainer: They put Saul's armor in the temple of the Ashtaroth and fasten his body to the wall of Beth-shan. The king's body is displayed as a trophy of war.

verses.text: When the inhabitants of Jabesh Gilead heard what the Philistines had done to Saul,
verses.explainer: When the inhabitants of Jabesh-gilead hear what the Philistines have done to Saul, they act. These are the people Saul saved in his first great victory.

crossReferences.connection: Saul's first act as king was rescuing Jabesh-gilead from Nahash the Ammonite. Now the people of Jabesh-gilead repay that debt by rescuing Saul's body. Loyalty bookends Saul's story.

verses.text: all the valiant men arose, went all night, and took the body of Saul and the bodies of his sons from the wall of Beth Shan; and they came to Jabesh, and burned them there.
verses.explainer: All the valiant men of Jabesh-gilead march through the night to Beth-shan. They take the bodies of Saul and his sons from the wall and bring them back to Jabesh, where they burn them.

thenVsNow.then: The men of Jabesh-gilead's action is one of the most moving examples of loyalty in Scripture. They remember a thirty-year-old debt of gratitude and risk everything to repay it. Saul's first act of heroism is remembered and honored even after his final disgrace.
thenVsNow.now: The men of Jabesh-gilead's action is one of the most moving examples of loyalty in Scripture. They remember a thirty-year-old debt of gratitude and risk everything to repay it. Saul's first act of heroism is remembered and honored even after his final disgrace.

verses.text: They took their bones and buried them under the tamarisk tree in Jabesh, and fasted seven days.
verses.explainer: They take the bones and bury them under the tamarisk tree in Jabesh and fast for seven days. The seven-day fast is the fullest expression of mourning.

thenVsNow.then: 1 Samuel ends not with David's triumph but with Saul's burial. The book refuses to reduce Saul to a simple villain. He is mourned by people who remember his best moments. The tragedy is real and the grief is genuine. Even a failed king deserves dignity in death.
thenVsNow.now: 1 Samuel ends not with David's triumph but with Saul's burial. The book refuses to reduce Saul to a simple villain. He is mourned by people who remember his best moments. The tragedy is real and the grief is genuine. Even a failed king deserves dignity in death.
